coredata.dc:title: Controlling thermal runaway by simultaneous use of thermoelectric module and phase change material in the lithium-ion batteries of electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Alghamdi, Hisham
Rosdi, Mohd Farhan Mat
Mukhtar, Azfarizal
Yasir, Ahmad Shah Hizam Md
Alviz-Meza, Aníbal
coredata.dc:description: This work examines the experimental investigation of different kinds of thermal management systems (TMSs) for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles. The investigation primarily focused on the battery thermal management system (BTMS) utilizing phase change material (PCM). The addition of the thermoelectric cooling system subsequently improved the BTMS. The findings indicate that when employing just PCM, the mean temperature reaches 85 °C, beyond the acceptable temperature range for battery safety. The use of the thermoelectric module in conjunction with the PCM has resulted in an elevation of the mean temperature of the battery to 76 °C within the specified timeframe. This outcome serves as a testament to the efficacy of the thermoelectric cooling system in further diminishing the average temperature of the battery. However, it should be noted that the battery's temperature remains within a hazardous threshold, thereby increasing the likelihood of thermal runaway and compromising the battery's overall performance and safety. The implementation of aluminum circular fins on the battery has resulted in a reduction in the average temperature from 76 °C to 65 °C. The alteration of the fin configuration from circular to axial fins resulted in the attainment of an average temperature of 48 °C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric vehicles
Lithium-ion battery (LIB)
Thermoelectric cooling
Phase change material (PCM)
Battery thermal management system (BTMS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the proposed BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module.
constructed model of BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module.
Schematic of the proposed BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module. (a) with circular fins on the battery, (b) with axial fins on the battery.
Different LIB configurations, (a) with axial fins, (b) with circular fins and (c) without fins.
Temperature of the battery body at the top and middle of the battery.
The body temperature of side batteries and middle battery for heat production mode is 9 W.
Changes in the temperature of the LIB over time, in different states of thermal power generation, for the battery with the PCM-TMS.
Changes in the temperature of the LIB over time, in different states of thermal power generation, for the battery with the PCM-TMS along with the thermoelectric cooling system.
Changes in the temperature of the LIB over time, in different states of thermal power generation, for the battery with circular fins inside the PCM along with the thermoelectric cooling system.
Changes in the temperature of the LIB over time, in different states of thermal power generation, for the battery with axial fins inside the PCM along with the thermoelectric cooling system.
Changes in the temperature of the LIB over time, in the production of thermal power of 15 W, for four different cases.
Average battery temperature in 100 min for different cases.
Duration of constant battery temperature during PCM melting in different cases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Uncertainty analysis of the proposed system for the mode of 9 W in 80 min.

head.ce:title: Controlling thermal runaway by simultaneous use of thermoelectric module and phase change material in the lithium-ion batteries of electric vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work examines the experimental investigation of different kinds of thermal management systems (TMSs) for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles. The investigation primarily focused on the battery thermal management system (BTMS) utilizing phase change material (PCM). The addition of the thermoelectric cooling system subsequently improved the BTMS. The findings indicate that when employing just PCM, the mean temperature reaches 85 °C, beyond the acceptable temperature range for battery safety. The use of the thermoelectric module in conjunction with the PCM has resulted in an elevation of the mean temperature of the battery to 76 °C within the specified timeframe. This outcome serves as a testament to the efficacy of the thermoelectric cooling system in further diminishing the average temperature of the battery. However, it should be noted that the battery's temperature remains within a hazardous threshold, thereby increasing the likelihood of thermal runaway and compromising the battery's overall performance and safety. The implementation of aluminum circular fins on the battery has resulted in a reduction in the average temperature from 76 °C to 65 °C. The alteration of the fin configuration from circular to axial fins resulted in the attainment of an average temperature of 48 °C.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, there exists a significant threat to both human life and various other organisms across the globe. Some significant hazards that pose a threat to human existence are global warming, environmental pollution [1], energy scarcity, and water scarcity [2,3]. All of these hazards can be attributed to the consequences of human actions and choices that have transpired over the course of history. One of the primary contributors to these hazards is the utilization of fossil fuels, which invariably leads to the phenomenon of global warming alongside the generation of perilous emissions [4,5]. The necessity of fossil fuels is intricately connected to both the direct and indirect aspects of human existence. The utilization of electricity is an indisputable necessity in human existence, with the majority of this energy being consistently generated within power facilities. The majority of power plants utilize fossil fuels as their primary source of energy for electricity generation. In addition to the human demand for power, there has been a concurrent rise in the number of automobiles due to population growth [6]. The majority of these cars use fossil fuels to power them, which increases emissions. The utilization of this particular energy source is associated with the phenomenon of global warming, mostly attributed to the significant proportion of carbon dioxide gas present in its emissions [7]. Moreover, it should be noted that fossil fuel sources are classified as non-renewable and hence lack the capacity to serve as a reliable and sustainable energy source. In light of these instances, the exploration of alternate sources of fossil fuels is imperative for the sustained existence of human life and other organisms.

In recent times, there has been a notable emergence of electric vehicles as a prospective alternative that effectively fulfills prevailing transportation requirements, curtails fuel use, and promotes ecological sustainability [8]. Electric vehicles are propelled by batteries, which necessitate the input of electrical energy for the purpose of recharging these batteries. Hence, it is imperative to ensure the provision of electric energy from a source that not only exhibits zero pollution [9] but also addresses the issue of insufficient electric energy supply. Renewable energy sources have been extensively discussed and analyzed in numerous academic papers as a dependable alternative. Solar energy is widely recognized as a significant and pervasive form of renewable energy, readily accessible in numerous regions across the globe in the form of both light and heat. The conversion of solar energy into various forms of energy, particularly electrical energy, is crucial for meeting the majority of human energy requirements [10]. Once solar energy has been converted into electrical energy, it can either be directly utilized or stored, depending on the immediate requirements. Batteries are a viable method for the storage of electrical energy.

Batteries play a pivotal and conspicuous role in electric and hybrid vehicles [11], serving as the primary source of power and fulfilling the critical function of electrochemical energy storage for these vehicles. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have garnered significant attention from industrialists and researchers worldwide due to their exceptional characteristics [12], making them a focal point of interest among other battery types. These batteries are extensively utilized in the realm of electric automobiles. The batteries possess notable characteristics such as increased energy density [13], reduced weight, and extended cycle life, making them widely recognized as the preferred energy storage solution for electric cars [14]. Despite the current commercialization of these batteries, a multitude of researchers worldwide are actively engaged in investigating methods to enhance their performance and augment their efficiency. The batteries exhibit their highest level of performance within the temperature range of 15–40° Celsius [15]. Furthermore, it should be noted that LIBs are prone to experiencing significant thermal runaway issues in the event of mechanical impact. Thermal runaway in LIBs refers to the phenomenon wherein the battery temperature exceeds the intended operational range [16]. There are several factors that can contribute to this occurrence, such as inadequate charging, excessive discharge, physical impact, mechanical harm, manufacturing flaws, or a functional anomaly inside the battery. When a LIB experience overheating, there is a significant acceleration in the chemical reactions occurring within the battery, resulting in an increase in electrical current flow and subsequent heat generation. In the occurrence of unregulated increase in temperature, there is a possibility of a concomitant elevation in the internal tension of the battery, finally leading to thermal runaway. The occurrence of thermal runaway in LIBs can result in significant ramifications, such as detrimental effects on battery cells, heightened potential for explosion and fire, and, in certain instances, adverse impacts on the surrounding ecosystem [17]. Hence, the regulation of temperature and mitigation of thermal runaway are of utmost significance.

In order to mitigate the occurrence of thermal runaway, LIBs are commonly outfitted with thermal management systems (TMSs) [18,19]. The implementation of a battery thermal management system (BTMS) is of utmost significance for rechargeable batteries, and it is widely employed in intricate electronic products, including laptops, mobile phones, and electric vehicles [20]. These systems employ constant monitoring of battery temperature and, when deemed required, implement appropriate actions to regulate the temperature of the battery. The aforementioned systems encompass a range of components, such as sensors, control circuits, and cooling systems. The battery temperature is monitored by sensors, which subsequently alert control mechanisms in the event of a temperature increase. The control circuits aim to manage the electric current flow to ensure that the battery temperature remains within the designated range. In the event of a need for temperature reduction, cooling systems have the capability to achieve this by means of heat transfer to the surrounding cooling environment [21]. Furthermore, the appropriate battery design and utilization of appropriate thermal absorption materials can also prove to be efficacious in mitigating thermal runaway [22,23]. In addition, adherence to appropriate usage circumstances, such as avoiding exposure of the battery to extreme temperatures and refraining from using damaged or unsuitable batteries, can also prove beneficial in mitigating the occurrence of thermal runaway [24]. Thermal runaway in LIBs is a significant concern, necessitating careful consideration of thermal management to ensure both safety and good battery performance [25].

Numerous investigations have been undertaken in the realm of thermal management pertaining to LIBs [26]. In recent years, there has been a predominant research emphasis on the utilization of phase change materials (PCMs) for BTMSs [27,28]. Zhang et al. [29] introduced a novel hybrid BTMS that utilizes a combination of PCM and liquid cooling. The maintenance of the battery's operating temperature in the proposed system primarily relies on the utilization of the latent heat of the PCM. During the thermal runaway mode, the PCM acts as a thermal buffer, helping heat to escape through the liquid cooling system so that less thermal runaway emissions are released. In their study, Mehrabi et al. [30] proposed a novel hybrid TMS for LIBs. This system incorporates PCMs in conjunction with forced air convection to effectively regulate the temperature of the batteries. In their study, Jiang et al. [31] presented a TMS with a sandwich structure composed of a battery, PCM, and heat pipe. In their study, Goud et al. [32] presented a thermal management model for LIBs that utilizes a non-paraffinic organic PCM. They conducted a comparative analysis of this model's performance against the conventional natural convection cooling mode.

According to the studies, thermal management of LIBs is very important to prevent thermal runaway. Furthermore, the BTMS must possess the capability to effectively lower the temperature of the battery within the permissible range, while also exhibiting a high level of dependability and actively functioning. Additionally, it is imperative that the design minimize spatial requirements and refrain from introducing any additional weight to the electric vehicle. This work presents a proposed model for a BTMS that incorporates both PCMs and thermoelectric modules. The present solution is both integrated and compact, occupying a smaller physical footprint compared to an electric vehicle. The utilization of PCMs can effectively mitigate the occurrence of rapid temperature escalation in batteries. This is primarily attributed to the inherent property of PCM to maintain a consistent temperature during the phase transition process. Moreover, when employed in an optimized manner, PCM can sustain a stable temperature within the battery for a certain duration. The absorption of a significant amount of heat from PCM and subsequent reduction in its melting rate can be attributed to the cooling effect exerted by thermoelectric modules. Additionally, for enhanced efficacy in battery temperature regulation, it is recommended to employ fins possessing both circular and axial structures. The subsequent section presents an instance of the BTMS that has been constructed using a battery simulator and afterwards evaluated under various configurations. Various configurations are being examined in terms of battery temperature regulation in order to implement a more efficient solution.

For the purpose of evaluating the efficacy of the proposed model in controlling the battery temperature, four distinct configurations of a BTMS have been created. The initial configuration employs only PCM. To achieve this, the batteries are enclosed in a plastic (polycarbonate) enclosure measuring 10 cm wide by 12 cm long by 15 cm high, and the surrounding space is completely filled with PCM. The batteries are placed in the middle of this compartment (three batteries) with a 5 mm distance between them in the longitudinal orientation. In the constructed model, a battery simulator is used in place of a battery to control the battery's heat and generate various heat generation modes. The PCM specifications are listed in Table 1 . The study by Le Jiang et al. [33] was used to select this PCM.

In the second configuration, thermoelectric modules are also employed in addition to PCM. For this purpose, thermoelectric modules are mounted in the walls of the battery compartment so that their cold side is in contact with the PCM inside the tank and their hot side is cooled by natural convection in the surrounding environment. Fig. 1 depicts the proposed model's schematic representation. Observably, the PCM material absorbs the heat produced by the batteries, and the thermoelectric modules then transfer this heat to the surrounding environment. Fig. 2 displays the constructed model. As depicted in this diagram, thermoelectric modules are installed on both sides of 10-cm-wide walls. The batteries are spaced 5 mm apart in the exact center of the chamber and along the longitudinal axis.

The subsequent configurations are comparable to the second configuration, with the exception that the battery body is attached to the fin. In the third configuration (Fig. 3 -a), circular fins are utilized, whereas in the fourth configuration (Fig. 3-b), axial fins are utilized.

As previously stated, a battery simulator has been utilized in place of a battery during experimental testing. For this purpose, a model with an identical geometric structure to LIB model 26500 was created. Its body is made of aluminum, and it contains a 12-V cartridge electric heater with a maximum output of 15 W. Completely filling the interior of the battery simulator's body with silicone paste facilitates the transmission of heat from the heater to the exterior surface of the battery simulator's body. The control system is able to adjust the thermal power generated by the battery simulator within a range of 2–15 W. This battery measures 26 mm in diameter and 50 mm in height.

In the constructed systems, the TEC1-12706 thermoelectric module is utilized (Fig. 4 -a). This module has dimensions of 40 mm by 40 mm by 4 mm and is powered by 12 V of DC current. This module's maximal cooling output is 50 W, which can be reduced by adjusting the module's input voltage. Utilizing aluminum heat sinks has increased the intensity of heat transfer. Its cold side is connected to a 40 × 40 mm aluminum heatsink with a 10-mm-tall fin on its cold side. Considering that the heated side of the thermoelectric module must be cooled by natural convection, a larger heatsinks is utilized on the hot side. This heatsink has dimensions of 150 mm in length, 40 mm in breadth, and 20 mm in fin height. Between the heatsink and thermoelectric module, a silicone paste has been applied to enhance heat transfer and reduce thermal resistance. Fig. 4-b depicts the arrangement of this thermoelectric module and its heatsinks. Also, the specifications of the components of the experimental model are presented in Table 2 .

In this study, models were evaluated using an NTC100 model thermometer with an accuracy of 0.1° Celsius and a temperature measurement range of −40 to 125° Celsius. This thermometer has measured temperature with high precision and over a broad range. Moreover, a multimeter with an accuracy of 0.01 amps and 0.01 V was used to measure the current intensity and voltage passing through the battery simulator heater and thermoelectric module. This multimeter has been capable of measuring current and voltage with great precision. Using this high-precision equipment, the TMS models for LIBs in electric vehicles were tested and the results were analyzed. These devices provide precise information regarding the performance of the BTMS and the influence of the thermoelectric module on temperature control, along with the requisite accuracy and range of measurement. The specifications of the measuring equipment are reported in Table 3 .

The experimental test has also been subjected to uncertainty analysis. In this investigation, statistical methods were employed to analyze temperature, current, and voltage data uncertainty. First, the measurement equipment's collected data was analyzed. The uncertainty in temperature, current, and voltage data was then computed using statistical methodologies such as standard deviation and variance [34,35]. Equations (1)–(4) have been utilized for this purpose.
(1)
A V R =
∑
j = 1
n
x j
n
(2)
SD =
∑
j = 1
n
(
x j
− A V R
)
2
(
n − 1
)
(3)
u =
SD
n
(4)
U = k
u c

The average value of the data is denoted as AVR. The parameter evaluated in each experiment is represented by the variable x. The number of experiments is denoted as n. The standard deviation is denoted as SD. The uncertainty is represented by the variable u. The combined uncertainty, taking into account the coverage factor k, is denoted as U. The equality of the value of K is deemed to be 2. The uncertainty analysis was performed on the data collected from the initial test in order to determine the uncertainty for each device. To fulfill the objective, the first experiment was replicated multiple times, and the resulting data, together with their corresponding uncertainty values, are presented in Table 4 . The findings indicate that, after conducting four repetitions for each mode in the test, the temperature uncertainty is determined to be 4.3 %, the current uncertainty is determined to be 0.58 %, and the voltage uncertainty is determined to be 0.95 %. The findings of this study indicate that the level of uncertainty in temperature, current, and voltage measurements, as observed during four repeats of the test, falls within a dependable range and can be considered reliable. Given that the level of uncertainty falls under an acceptable threshold, it is justifiable to utilize the data derived from the analysis with a higher degree of confidence, so establishing the validity and reliability of the obtained results.

In this experimental investigation, four distinct BTMS models were constructed, and each was evaluated experimentally. The constructed models include the following: a BTMS with PCM, a BTMS with PCM, and a thermoelectric module, a BTMS with PCM and a thermoelectric module and circular fins on the battery, and a BTMS with PCM and a thermoelectric module and axial fins on the battery. Various battery configurations are depicted in Fig. 5 . In each of the aforementioned models, a battery simulator was employed in lieu of an actual battery. The battery simulator was equipped with an electric heater capable of adjusting its thermal power within the range of 2–15 W. Three battery simulators were utilized in each constructed model, strategically positioned within the central region of the chamber, maintaining a separation distance of 5 mm between each simulator. Every model produced undergoes testing under three distinct heat production situations. In the initial scenario, the aggregate thermal energy generated by three battery simulators amounted to 9 W. In the second and third instances, the quantity of thermal energy generated was equivalent to 15 and 30 W, correspondingly. The temperature of the battery surface and the current and voltage intensities flowing through the heater and thermoelectric were monitored and recorded in each experimental trial. Temperature measurements were taken and documented at regular intervals of 5 min, commencing from the initiation of the experiment and continuing until 100 min had elapsed since the commencement. The subsequent section presents a discussion of the outcomes obtained from the experimental test.

Experiments were conducted on the constructed model. The battery cell compartment was initially filled with PCM material and evaluated. The temperature sensor was strategically positioned at two distinct locations within the battery, namely the top and middle regions, to accurately gauge the temperature in those specific areas. Based on the data presented in Fig. 6 , it can be observed that the temperature of the battery body is consistently greater in the top region compared to the middle region across all modes and time intervals. In order to assess the temperature of the battery body in various stages, it is necessary to consider the temperature of the top portion of the battery. Given that the temperature in this particular region is at its peak, it is imperative to regulate the maximum temperature of the battery.

As stated, the constructed experimental model is comprised of three batteries. At boundary conditions, the two side batteries are equivalent, whereas their conditions differ from those of the middle battery. As a result, in order to ascertain the critical state, the body temperatures of a side battery and a middle battery were compared. It was determined, as shown in Fig. 7 , that the middle battery has a significantly higher temperature than the side batteries. The reason for this is that the temperature of the side batteries is more precisely regulated due to their proximity to the thermoelectric modules. As a consequence, for the continuation of this investigation, solely the temperature of the middle battery body was chosen to be examined further.

As shown in Fig. 8 , experiments were conducted for three distinct modes of battery heat generation. Throughout the procedure, the ambient temperature remained nearly constant at 25° Celsius. In the absence of the thermoelectric cooling system for PCM, it was observed that the temperature of the battery initially rises, and then rises gently over a period of time, for all three thermal powers of 9, 15, and 30 W. It continues, and as more time passes, the temperature rises again with greater intensity, before reaching a steady state through a gradual process. The initial increase in temperature is due to the fact that the PCM material has not yet reached its melting point temperature; as the battery heats up, the PCM material's temperature rises, and as a result, the battery body's temperature also rises. Due to the change in state and the formation of a temperature profile, the temperature tends to remain constant once the PCM begins to melt. In reality, however, the PCMs surrounding the battery melt first, and this process continues in the direction of the battery's radius until all PCMs are completely melted. Since it is impossible for all PCM material to begin melting simultaneously, the PCMs surrounding the battery begin to melt and heat up first. However, because the PCMs of the front layers are still melting, they resist the increase in temperature and cause the batteries' body temperature to rise very gradually so long as not all PCMs in the battery compartment have melted.

According to Fig. 8, as predicted, the initial inclination of the initial increase in battery body temperature has a direct correlation with the power value. As battery power increases, so does the slope of temperature variations. At 9 W, the temperature of the battery continues to rise over a prolonged period of time and reaches a stable state at 69° Celsius after 80 min. After approximately 100 min in the 15-W heat production mode, the battery reaches a sustained temperature of 86° Celsius, which is significantly higher than the 9-W mode. In the case of 30 W, the temperature rises with a very steep slope and reaches its optimum of 96° Celsius after 100 min. The reason for this is that, at higher thermal powers, PCM absorbs more heat per unit of time, causing it to melt more quickly. It is anticipated that quicker melting will expedite the process of temperature increase. Based on these findings, it cannot be assumed that the PCM alone can regulate the battery temperature.

The use of thermoelectric cooling modules is suggested to enhance the impact of PCMs on the temperature regulation of batteries. Therefore, a model was created and examined. Fig. 9 illustrates that the use of the thermoelectric module improved the temperature rise behavior of the batteries. As can be seen, the increase in temperature is less severe in all circumstances and at all times compared to the previous situation. During the 9 W mode of power generation, the battery temperature remained constant at 40.5° Celsius for approximately 10 min. In the subsequent 70 min, the temperature increased with a very low slope and attained a steady state. In the mode without thermoelectric cooling, the temperature of the battery stabilizes after 80 min. Moreover, in the absence of any cooling system, the system attains thermal equilibrium at a temperature of 69° Celsius. Conversely, when the thermoelectric cooling system is employed, the battery achieves thermal stability at a lower temperature of 55° Celsius. This observation suggests that the implementation of the thermoelectric cooling system resulted in a reduction of the battery's temperature by around 20.2 %. In the power generation modes of 15 and 30 W, the battery did not experience a constant temperature, but the intensity of the temperature rise has diminished. After 100 min, the maximum temperature for thermal capacities of 15 and 30 W was 76 and 89° Celsius, respectively, a reduction of 11.6 % and 9.6 %, respectively, compared to the state without a cooling system.

Even though the use of thermoelectric modules enhances the cooling process, the maximum temperature of the battery after 100 min in the thermal power mode of 15 W is still 76° Celsius. This temperature is outside the operational temperature range of the battery, and attaining this temperature reduces the battery's performance and increases the risk of thermal runaway and even explosion. This is because the thermoelectric module is a heat absorber, but it is challenging to transfer heat from the battery side to the module. To facilitate this transmission, methods that enhance heat transfer must be implemented.

To increase the efficiency of thermoelectric modules in PCM cooling, it is suggested that circular fins be placed around the battery to improve heat transfer. In addition to increasing the rate of heat transmission, this action can cause the fins to penetrate deeply into the PCM, thereby facilitating a more uniform melting of the PCM. Fig. 10 demonstrates that, as the battery temperature rises, after the PCM reaches the melting point temperature, the battery temperature remains constant for a period of time in all three phases, and then increases with a gentle slope. Therefore, it is evident that the fins contribute to the homogenous melting of PCM, preventing layer-by-layer melting. Uniform melting maintains the temperature within the range of the melting point for longer.

As shown in Fig. 10, the battery temperature remains constant for 60, 55, and 35 min in the 9, 15, and 30 W modes, respectively. At high powers, the temperature of the battery remains constant for a lesser period of time because, as the power increases, so does the rate at which PCM melts. It can also be observed that in the 30 W power mode, the battery rapidly reaches the PCM melting point temperature, which is the cause of the increase in heat transfer rate. After 100 min, the maximal temperature of the battery for 9, 15, and 30 W was 50, 65, and 84° Celsius, respectively. Compared to the case without fins, these temperatures have decreased by 9 %, 14 %, and 5.6 %, indicating an improvement in the battery cooling process. In general, the use of crucibles enhances the rate of heat transfer from the battery to the thermoelectric module as a result of the increased heat transfer surface, and also results in a more uniform melting of the PCM. A small amount of PCM melting also allows the battery to maintain a constant temperature for an extended period of time. Despite this, it can be concluded that the use of ring fins at high powers had little effect on the improvement of battery temperature reduction, and in the mode of producing 30 W of thermal power, it was only able to reduce battery temperature by 5.6 % more than in the absence of fins. Because the surface of circular fins is perpendicular to the vertical direction, their use is detrimental to heat transfer via natural convection. Despite the fact that the heat transmission is enhanced by an increase in surface area, they reduce natural convection. As a result of these two factors, the use of circular fins has little impact on the cooling process of batteries.

A different arrangement of fins has been proposed to enhance the cooling process of LIBs using PCM and thermoelectric cooling systems. This time, to enhance heat transfer, axial fins were utilized, as opposed to circular fins in the past. As shown in Fig. 11 , the use of axial fins to cool batteries has been extremely effective. The findings suggest that the battery temperature quickly achieves a value of 41°, equivalent to the melting point of the PCM, and afterwards maintains a steady state for a prolonged duration. After the complete melting of the PCM, the temperature of the battery increases with a very low slope over time for all modes. Fig. 11 indicates that, for the 9 W mode of power generation, after the battery temperature reaches the PCM melting point, the temperature of the battery remains constant and approximately equal to 44° Celsius until the end of the test period. It turns out that at this temperature it reaches a stable state. The same holds true for the 15 W mode of power generation. In the 15W mode, however, the battery achieves the melting point temperature faster, and after 100 min, it reaches a temperature of 49° Celsius, at which point it is considered to have reached its stable state.

The maximum battery temperature for the thermal power modes of 9, 15, and 30 W was depicted in Fig. 11 as 44, 49, and 53° Celsius, respectively. The use of axial fins with the same cross-section as circular fins reduces battery temperature by 12 %, 24.6 %, and 36.9 %, respectively, when compared to circular fins. In addition to the advantages mentioned for annular fins, the use of axial fins also enhances natural convection, which aids in enhancing heat transfer and uniform heat distribution. At high thermal powers, it has also been observed that the battery's performance improves more than in other modes, which can be attributed to the increase in natural convection heat transfer rate at high powers.

On the battery, four distinct modes were compared: BTMS with PCM, BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module, BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module and circular fins, and BTMS with PCM and thermoelectric module with axial fins. The total thermal power produced by the batteries across all four modes was 15 W. As shown in Fig. 12 , under identical environmental conditions, the temperature of the battery initially rises to the PCM's melting point in all instances. For modes without fins, in the absence of a thermoelectric cooling system as well as in the presence of a thermoelectric cooling system, the battery does not experience a constant temperature, but its temperature rises with a very low gradient. It can also be observed that the mode with thermoelectric cooling system has been more effective at regulating battery temperature than the mode without cooling. In instances where fins are utilized, both the use of circular fins and axial fins have had a significant impact on regulating battery temperature. Additionally, it has been observed that axial fins are more effective than circular fins. It is evident from Fig. 12 that the use of thermoelectric modules has a significant impact on battery cooling. Due to the fact that by utilizing electrical energy, it absorbs heat and transfers it to the surrounding environment, thereby accelerating the rate of heat transfer. Additionally, the use of fins has a greater effect on the heat transfer rate due to the increase in the heat transfer surface, while the use of axial fins, in addition to increasing the surface, also increases the convection heat transfer rate, resulting in the cooling of the battery, it is superior.

Fig. 13 depicts a comparison of the average temperature over 100 min for these four distinct circumstances. The results indicate that the average temperature for the TMS mode of battery temperature by PCM without a thermoelectric cooling system is 85° Celsius, which is significantly higher than the battery's safe operating temperature range. This average temperature over 100 min demonstrates that relying solely on PCM to manage battery temperature is inappropriate and can result in overheating and even explosions. Using the thermoelectric module in conjunction with the PCM has enabled the battery's average temperature to rise to 76° Celsius during this period, demonstrating the efficiency of the thermoelectric cooling system in further reducing the battery's average temperature. However, the battery's temperature is still within a hazardous range, where thermal runaway and diminished battery performance and safety are possible. The use of aluminum ring fins on the battery has allowed the average temperature to increase from 76° Celsius to 65° Celsius, indicating that these fins, in conjunction with the PCM and the thermoelectric cooling system, can maintain the battery within a safe temperature range. By altering the arrangement of fins from circular to axial, the average temperature of the LIB was observed to reach 48° Celsius, indicating an effective TMS for temperature control.

Fig. 14 depicts the temporal variation of battery temperature, revealing noteworthy findings. If the cooling system is not utilized, the temperature of the battery within the initial 15-min period following the initiation of discharge falls into the temperature range at which the phase change of the PCM material occurs, transitioning from a solid to a liquid state. Over the course of this time period, there is a progressive increase in the temperature of the battery. The observed rise in temperature could perhaps be attributed to inadequate regulation of battery temperature and heightened heat production during the discharge process. The inclusion of a thermoelectric device in the overall system reveals that as the battery temperature nears the range at which the PCM melts, there is a notable impact on temperature regulation. This implies that the temperature of the battery is regulated to remain within the melting temperature range of the PCM for a duration of 30 min following the initiation of discharge. The decrease in temperature can potentially lead to enhanced thermal efficiency and more precise temperature regulation within the system. The utilization of annular fins and axial fins as a cooling system leads to a more pronounced manifestation of this phenomenon. The duration for which the battery temperature remains within the range of the PCM melting temperature is extended to 65 and 85 min, correspondingly. The observed prolongation of duration can be attributed to the enhanced thermal control capabilities of the cooling system, which effectively mitigates the occurrence of abrupt temperature surges during battery discharge. The findings of this study demonstrate the significance of incorporating a cooling mechanism and implementing effective heat management strategies in LIBs. Based on the observed results in the current investigation, it can be concluded that this strategy has the potential to effectively enhance battery performance and prolong their useable lifespan.

Due to the fact that the temperature of the PCM remains constant during the phase change, the precise application of PCMs can be very useful for controlling the temperature of LIBs. If the system can be designed so that the PCM material experiences a more uniform melting, then the batteries can maintain a constant temperature for an extended period of time. In addition, the use of thermoelectric modules permits the majority of the heat produced by the batteries to be removed from the PCM, thereby delaying the PCM's melting. The results demonstrated that thermoelectric modules can aid in temperature regulation. Additionally, it was discovered that the optimal performance was achieved when the batteries were connected to the axial fins. The more uniform melting of PCMs can be credited with this outstanding performance.

Based on the findings of this investigation, it is evident that relying solely on the implementation of PCM as a BTMS is insufficient to maintain the battery's average temperature below a safe threshold. The incorporation of the thermoelectric module into the system resulted in a notable reduction in the average temperature of the battery, ultimately reaching a value of 76° Celsius. Nevertheless, the current battery temperature remains under a hazardous threshold, so increasing the likelihood of thermal runaway and compromising both battery performance and safety.

The implementation of aluminum circular fins, in conjunction with PCM and a thermoelectric cooling system, resulted in a notable decrease in the average battery temperature to 65° Celsius. The alteration of the fin configuration from a circular arrangement to an axial configuration resulted in an average temperature of 48° Celsius being attained. The findings of this study indicate that the utilization of axial fins has superior efficacy in regulating the temperature of LIBs compared to ring fins.

Additionally, an investigation was conducted to examine the duration during which the battery temperature remained consistent. In the absence of a cooling system, the battery's temperature remains within the range of the melting point of the PCM for a duration of 15 min, after which its temperature begins to rise. The inclusion of a thermoelectric system results in a time extension of up to 30 min. The durations for the modes of circular fins and axial fins are 65 and 85 min, respectively.

Furthermore, empirical evidence suggests that the temperature at the uppermost region of the battery exceeds that of the middle section. An advantageous feature of employing a heatsink is the attainment of a more uniform temperature distribution across the battery. This uniformity serves to prevent localized melting of the PCM and promotes a more homogeneous melting process.

The findings of this study demonstrate that the implementation of a BTMS incorporating PCM and thermoelectric cooling, along with circular or axial fins, can effectively mitigate battery temperature, enhance safety and performance, and prevent thermal runaway and associated damage.
Hisham Alghamdi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft, Visualization.
Mohd Farhan Mat Rosdi: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - Review & Editing.
Azfarizal Mukhtar: Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing.
Ahmad Shah Hizam Md Yasir: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing.
Aníbal Alviz-Meza: Project administration, Visualization, Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the Research Groups Funding program grant code (NU/RG/SERC/12/16).
